examples: He neva know how for spell punnami.
missing.usage: A common misspelling of 'punani' (vagina). Often used in casual or vulgar island slang.

english: Take care of your private parts
examples: Brah, you bettah malama your boto!
missing.usage: A humorous or warning phrase often said between friends. 'Malama' means to take care of, and 'boto' refers to male genitalia.

examples: Ole way! I forgot my slippahs!
missing.usage: A phonetic misspelling of 'auwe' (oh my, alas). Expresses shock, sadness, or disappointment.

examples: He was all bline last night at da party.
missing.usage: Short for 'blind.' Often used in the phrase 'all bline' to describe being extremely drunk or intoxicated.

examples: I almost pow with my work.
missing.usage: A common phonetic spelling of 'pau' (finished, done). Used when something is completed or over.

examples: Dis plate lunch stay so ono!
missing.usage: Used to describe food that is exceptionally good. 'Ono' is the Hawaiian word for delicious.
A common misspelling of 'Hawaiian.' Used to refer to the people, language, or culture of Hawaii.
Common responses include 'howzit', 'stay good', 'shoots', or simply a head nod. It is more of a greeting than a question requiring a detailed answer.
